00-inbox/asap-sim-to-real.title: ASAP: Aligning Simulation and Real-World Physics for Learning Agile Humanoid Whole-Body Skills
00-inbox/asap-sim-to-real.content:
Summary
Domain randomization for humanoid sim-to-real transfer produces conservative policies that sacrifice agility; system identification is limited by its pre-defined parameter space and often requires unavailable torque measurements. ASAP trains a delta action model — a residual RL policy that learns corrective actions to close the sim-to-real gap — by replaying real-world trajectories in simulation and minimizing state discrepancy. The fine-tuned policy is then deployed without the delta model, achieving agile whole-body motions (1.5m forward jumps, single-leg balance, 180° mid-air rotations) on the Unitree G1, with up to 2× reduction in global MPJPE versus SysID and DeltaDynamics baselines.
Core Mechanism
Stage 1 — Pre-training. Motion tracking policy \pi_\theta is trained in IsaacGym via PPO. Human video → TRAM (3D pose estimation) → SMPL parameters → shape-and-motion retargeting to robot joint space. State includes 5-step history of joint positions, velocities, root angular velocity, projected gravity, and last action:
s^p_t \triangleq [q_{t-4:t},\; \dot{q}_{t-4:t},\; \omega^\text{root}_{t-4:t},\; g_{t-4:t},\; a_{t-5:t-1}]
Goal state is a scalar phase variable \phi \in [0,1] — no odometry or global position target, which eliminates a major sim-to-real failure mode. Asymmetric actor-critic: critic sees global reference positions and root linear velocity; actor only sees s^p_t and \phi. Two training tricks matter critically:

Termination curriculum: initial tolerance 1.5m → tightened to 0.3m over training. Prevents the policy from learning to stand still to avoid landing penalty.
Reference State Initialization (RSI): episodes initialized at random \phi, robot state set to the corresponding reference frame. Allows parallel learning of all motion phases, critical for contact-rich agile motions.

Stage 2 — Delta action model. Deploy pre-trained \pi_\theta on real hardware; collect trajectory \mathcal{D}^r = \{s^r_0, a^r_0, \ldots, s^r_T, a^r_T\} via MoCap + onboard sensors. Replay (s^r_t, a^r_t) in simulation; discrepancy s_{t+1} - s^r_{t+1} is the training signal.
The delta model \pi^\Delta_\theta(s_t, a_t) \to \Delta a_t is trained with RL to minimize this discrepancy:
s_{t+1} = f^\text{sim}(s_t,\; a^r_t + \Delta a_t) \approx s^r_{t+1}
Reward: state-matching terms (body position, joint position, angular velocity) + action magnitude regularization \exp(-\|a_t\|) - 1 to prevent degenerate large corrections. The RL episode is initialized at the real-world state s^r_t, so each step supervises the delta model with a real-world anchor.
Crucially: \pi^\Delta operates in the action space, not the state space. Compared to delta dynamics (\Delta s_{t+1} = f^\Delta_\theta(s_t, a_t)), delta actions are bounded by the physical reachability of the actuators, which prevents the cascading error accumulation that DeltaDynamics exhibits at long horizons.
Stage 2b — Fine-tuning. Freeze \pi^\Delta. Fine-tune \pi_\theta inside the augmented simulator:
f^\text{ASAP}(s_t, a_t) = f^\text{sim}(s_t,\; a_t + \pi^\Delta(s_t, a_t))
Same reward as pre-training. No delta model at deployment.
Results. At 1.0s rollout horizon in IsaacSim, ASAP achieves E_\text{g-mpjpe} = 37.9\text{mm} vs. OpenLoop 80.8mm, SysID 77.6mm, DeltaDynamics 68.1mm. On Genesis: 36.9 vs 82.5/76.5/50.2. Success rate 100% at Hard level across both simulators; DeltaDynamics drops to 66.7% (IsaacSim) and 60.0% (Genesis).
Why It Matters
This lets you train highly agile whole-body motions — the kind that require precise timing of contact forces during jumping and landing — in a single GPU simulator, then deploy them on hardware without re-tuning the policy for each real-world physics discrepancy. Previously, the tradeoff was: either use DR (safe but slow/conservative, because the randomized distribution must cover reality), or SysID (sharp but brittle if the real dynamics fall outside the identified parameter manifold, e.g., nonlinear motor saturation). The delta action model is a middle path: it learns a correction function in the action space rather than trying to identify physical parameters or predict full dynamics, which is both lower-dimensional and better regularized by physical plausibility.
Concrete bottleneck removed: prior work on expressive humanoid motions (ExBody, HumanPlus) achieved upper-body expressiveness but failed on lower-body agility because the DR-trained policies learned to minimize ground contact variation — exactly the behavior you need to unlearn for jumping. ASAP’s MoCap-anchored fine-tuning gives the policy real contact dynamics to reason about.
Limitations

MoCap dependency for data collection: real-world trajectory collection requires a motion capture system for base position/orientation. This limits deployment to labs with MoCap infrastructure; onboard state estimation at the quality needed for this supervision signal is unsolved.
Single-morphology delta model: \pi^\Delta is trained per-robot and per-simulator-pair. Transferring it to a different hardware (e.g., H1 vs. G1) requires full retraining of Stage 2.
Fixed-wrist hardware: real-world experiments use the G1 with fixed wrists, removing 6+ DoF from the most complex manipulation-locomotion interface. Whole-body agility including hand/finger control remains untested.
No explicit contact modeling: the delta action model learns implicit corrections for motor saturation and joint flexibility, but doesn’t model contact timing explicitly. If the real-world ground friction or compliance differs substantially from sim, the correction signal will be confounded.
Data volume unknown: the paper does not report how many real-world rollout trajectories were collected per motion, making it unclear how sample-efficient Stage 2 is in practice.

Open Questions

Can \pi^\Delta generalize across motions it wasn’t trained on, or does it need per-motion real-world data? The paper trains it on a distribution of motions but doesn’t ablate this.
The phase variable \phi works for single-motion tracking — how would this extend to interactive or reactive tasks where the phase is not pre-defined (catching, reactive stepping)?
The delta model is deployed at test time only during fine-tuning, not at inference. Would online adaptation of \pi^\Delta (updating it from the robot’s current session data) close the remaining gap further, and at what control-loop cost?
Can the SMPL→robot retargeting + simulation-based data cleaning step be replaced with a purely video-based imitation approach that bypasses explicit motion capture entirely?

Links

00-inbox/mob-net-torque-estimation.title: MOB-Net: Limb-modularized Uncertainty Torque Learning of Humanoids for Sensorless External Torque Estimation
00-inbox/mob-net-torque-estimation.content:
Summary
The momentum observer (MOB) estimates external joint torques without force/torque sensors (FTS), but its residual signal is corrupted by model uncertainty — lumped modeling error, nonlinear joint friction, and sensor noise — which makes it unusable for collision detection on walking humanoids. MOB-Net wraps the MOB residual inside a limb-modularized GRU architecture that learns to predict and subtract the uncertainty torque, leaving a clean external torque estimate with RMSE reduced from 19.30 Nm (MOB alone) to 1.31 Nm (near the sensor-noise floor) in simulation. The estimated torque and its predicted variance are then used for collision detection thresholding and three full collision-reaction scenarios on a walking full-size humanoid (TOCABI, 1.8m, 100kg).
Core Mechanism
MOB baseline. Floating-base dynamics in generalized coordinates (n+6 DoF including 6 virtual joints at the pelvis):
M(q_v)\ddot{q}_v + C(q_v, \dot{q}_v)\dot{q}_v + g(q_v) = \tau_v + \tau_e
Generalized momentum p = M(q_v)\dot{q}_v; its derivative is:
\dot{p} = \tau_v + \tau_e + \beta(q_v, \dot{q}_v), \quad \beta = C^\top \dot{q}_v - g(q_v)
The MOB residual vector (avoids noisy \ddot{q} by integrating):
r = K_0\!\left(p(t) - p(0) - \int_0^t (\tau_v + \beta + r)\,dt\right)
Per-joint transfer function: r_i(s)/\tau_{e,i}(s) = k_{0,i}/(s + k_{0,i}) — MOB is a first-order low-pass of the true external torque. In practice: r = \tilde\tau_e + \tilde\tau_u, where \tilde{\cdot} denotes low-pass filtered.
Uncertainty torque decomposition. Three additive sources:
\tau_u(q_v, \dot{q}_v, \ddot{q}_v, \tau_v) = \underbrace{\tau_p}_{\text{model error}} + \underbrace{\tau_f(q_v, \dot{q}_v, \tau_v)}_{\text{friction (incl. load-dep.)}} + \underbrace{\tau_n}_{\text{sensor noise}}
Leg friction is load-dependent (Stribeck + k_{lf}|\tau_m|^2 term) due to 100:1 harmonic gear under >1000N load; arm friction depends only on velocity. This asymmetry drives the modular architecture.
Problem formulation. Estimate the per-joint Gaussian distribution of uncertainty torque from proprioception X_p:
\hat\tau_u^*(X_p),\, \hat\sigma_u^{2*}(X_p) = \arg\max_{\hat\tau_u, \hat\sigma_u^2} P(\tau_u | \hat\tau_u, \hat\sigma_u, X_p)
Solved by minimizing Gaussian negative log-likelihood:
\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i,j}\left[\ln\hat\sigma^2_{u,j,\theta} + \frac{(\hat\tau_{u,j,\theta} - \tau_{u,j}^i)^2}{\hat\sigma^2_{u,j,\theta}}\right]
Output: \tilde\tau_e = r - \hat\tau_u (pure external torque). Variance \hat\sigma_u is used as an adaptive collision detection threshold.
Limb modularization. The inertia matrix M(q_v) is sparse: off-diagonal blocks are zero between non-adjacent limb chains. Six independent GRU-Linear networks, one per limb group g \in \{V, RL, LL, W, RA, LA\}, each receiving only kinematically relevant joints. Input per step:
x_g(k) = [Q_g(k),\; T_g(k),\; U^V(k)]
where Q_g = [q_g, \dot{q}_g], T_g = [\tau_d^g] (desired torque, legs only), U^V = [{}^p R_{fb,1:6}, \omega_{fb}, \dot{v}_{fb}] (pelvis IMU). Joint acceleration is dropped (too noisy); its information is recovered implicitly by the GRU’s hidden state over a 50-100 step TBPTT horizon. Total network parameters: ~777K across all six networks; inference <1ms on a single Intel i7 core.
Training data. Legs: random walking data on uneven terrain (wooden plates 10mm, 3° slope) + Random Torque Exploration (RTE) — step-function random torques up to 50Nm added to joints to cover the velocity/torque distributions encountered during collisions. Target: r - \tau_e^m (residual minus FTS-measured external torque). Upper body: free teleoperated motion, target: r directly (no contact). ~3.6M samples per set, 1kHz.
Collision detection. Threshold on |\tilde\tau_e| is replaced with a probability-based condition using the estimated variance: collision is flagged when |\tilde\tau_e| > \eta \cdot \hat\sigma_u for tunable \eta, making the sensitivity adaptive to the current uncertainty level rather than a fixed global threshold.
Why It Matters
This lets a humanoid detect and react to unexpected collisions during walking without FTS anywhere except at the feet for data collection — the feet FTS are needed for training supervision but not at deployment. Previously the two options were: (a) FTS on every link (expensive, adds inertia, fails at occluded contacts), or (b) model-based MOB alone (RMSE 19 Nm — unusable as a collision signal because ground reaction forces during heel-strike produce similar magnitudes). The variance output doubles as a detection threshold, so you don’t need to manually retune a fixed threshold for each terrain or gait mode — the network tells you how uncertain it is.
Concrete scenario enabled: a walking humanoid’s foot collides with a heavy box in a camera blind spot. The external torque estimate spikes at the foot/ankle virtual joints, the collision is detected within one control cycle, and a back-step reaction is executed. None of this was demonstrated on a full-size walking humanoid before this work.
Limitations

FTS still required for data collection on legs: the training target for leg joints is r - \tau_e^m where \tau_e^m comes from FTS. Platforms without foot FTS must use the hanging-robot alternative, which requires specialized infrastructure and ignores load-dependent friction.
Distribution shift at collision time: training data is collision-free (except ground reactions and RTE perturbations). RTE improves robustness but cannot cover all collision geometries; accuracy degrades for collisions that push joints outside the trained velocity/torque distribution — especially high-speed impacts.
No contact point estimation: the output is external torque in generalized coordinates. Mapping back to contact wrench in operational space requires solving an underdetermined system when contact location is unknown, which the paper does not address.
Fixed walking controller during data collection: all training data uses one specific walking controller. If a different gait or agile motion (jumping, running) is deployed, the uncertainty torque distribution changes and the network must be retrained.
Harmonic gear assumption: the load-dependent friction model targets high gear-ratio (100:1) actuators. For QDD or SEA actuators with near-zero friction, the leg input feature design (T_g included) may be unnecessary and the modular split may need revisiting.

Open Questions

Can the FTS supervision signal for leg training be replaced with contact force estimates from a separate foot contact estimator, making the whole pipeline truly sensor-free at both training and test time?
The RTE perturbations are heuristic (uniform random step functions up to 50Nm). Is there a principled way to design the exploration distribution — e.g., adversarially sampling torques that maximize estimation error on a held-out collision set?
How does MOB-Net compose with a whole-body controller that actively changes the desired torque \tau_d (like the ViC-MPC contact wrench optimizer)? The input T_g feeds current desired torque, so rapid MPC-driven torque changes might confuse the GRU’s hidden state.
The limb modularity exploits the sparsity of M(q_v) — but C(q_v, \dot{q}_v) has Coriolis terms that couple limbs during fast motions. At what motion speed does this approximation break, and can cross-limb attention (sparse transformer) recover the missing coupling without abandoning the real-time budget?

Links

00-inbox/optnet.title: OptNet: Differentiable Optimization as a Layer in Neural Networks
00-inbox/optnet.content:
Summary
Standard neural network layers cannot enforce hard constraints between hidden states. OptNet embeds a quadratic program (QP) as a differentiable layer, making the layer output the solution z^* of a constrained optimization problem parameterized by the preceding layer. Gradients flow through the layer via implicit differentiation of the KKT conditions, and a custom GPU-batched primal-dual interior point solver makes the backward pass essentially free once the forward solve is done.
Core Mechanism
The OptNet layer solves:
z_{i+1} = \arg\min_z \frac{1}{2} z^T Q(z_i) z + q(z_i)^T z \quad \text{s.t.} \quad A(z_i)z = b(z_i),\; G(z_i)z \leq h(z_i)
Backward pass via KKT differentiation. At the optimal (z^*, \nu^*, \lambda^*), differentiate the stationarity, primal feasibility, and complementary slackness conditions to get:
\begin{bmatrix} Q & G^T & A^T \\ D(\lambda^*)G & D(Gz^*-h) & 0 \\ A & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} dz \\ d\lambda \\ d\nu \end{bmatrix} = -\text{RHS}(dQ, dq, dG, dh, dA, db)
For backprop, solve the transpose of this system once against \partial \ell / \partial z^*, then read off all parameter gradients:
\nabla_Q \ell = \tfrac{1}{2}(dz\, z^{*T} + z^* dz^T), \quad \nabla_q \ell = dz, \quad \nabla_b \ell = -d\nu, \quad \nabla_h \ell = -D(\lambda^*)d\lambda, \;\ldots
Solver. A GPU-batched primal-dual interior point method (PDIPM) following Mattingley & Boyd. Each iteration solves:
K \begin{bmatrix} \Delta z^{\text{aff}} \\ \Delta s^{\text{aff}} \\ \Delta \lambda^{\text{aff}} \\ \Delta \nu^{\text{aff}} \end{bmatrix} = -r_{\text{KKT}}
The LU factorization of K_{\text{sym}} at convergence is reused for the backward pass linear solve — no second factorization required. Batch size 128, 100-variable QPs: 0.18s vs Gurobi’s 4.7s.
Representational power. OptNet \supseteq ReLU (can encode \max\{Wz+b, 0\} as a QP with O(mn) params). Conversely, \max\{a_1^T x, a_2^T x, a_3^T x\} is a single OptNet layer but requires O(c^p) ReLU units to approximate — ReLU creases span all of \mathbb{R}^n; QP feasibility region creases can be bounded.
Why It Matters
This lets you embed known hard constraints (physical feasibility, logical rules, safety bounds) directly into the computation graph where previously you had to either hand-engineer a post-processing projection (not differentiable end-to-end) or hope the network learned to satisfy them implicitly (it won’t). Concrete: in the Sudoku experiment, a 10-layer CNN with 512 filters per layer overfits and fails to generalize to unseen puzzles; a single OptNet layer with a learnable Ax=b recovers near-perfect generalization — because row/column/box exclusivity are hard linear constraints, not soft patterns.
Signal denoising: initialize the OptNet difference operator D to the TV solution, fine-tune end-to-end → 12% test MSE improvement. You couldn’t do this with a fixed convex program.
Limitations

Cubic scaling: QP solve is O(n^3) in variables/constraints. Practically limited to n < 1000; below 100 for real-time. This rules out any application with high-dimensional decision spaces.
Dense matrices only: The solver exploits GPU-batched dense BLAS. Real-world problems with sparse constraint structure (e.g., MPC with sparse dynamics) don’t benefit and are penalized by dense factorization overhead.
Training instability: The QP parameter manifold has gauge redundancies (scaling constraint rows doesn’t change the solution set), so gradient landscapes are degenerate. More tuning required than FC/conv layers.
Complementary slackness degeneracy: At points where \lambda_i^* = 0 and (Gz^*-h)_i = 0 simultaneously, the KKT matrix is singular — the layer is subdifferentiable but not differentiable, and gradient-based training becomes unreliable.

Open Questions

Can structured (sparse, banded) QP solvers be integrated while maintaining GPU batch efficiency? The authors mention cuSOLVER sparse QR as a start.
How does this interact with stochastic training when QP feasibility depends on learned parameters — are there initialization strategies that guarantee feasibility throughout training?
For MPC-in-the-loop robotics: can the O(n^3) barrier be broken by exploiting time-structured (block-banded) KKT systems from the MPC horizon?
Learnable Q: parameterizing via Cholesky Q = LL^T + \epsilon I ensures PSD but doubles the parameter count. Are there better parameterizations for structured physics-inspired Q?

Links
implicit-differentiation bilevel-optimization sensitivity-analysis model-predictive-control KKT-conditions interior-point-methods differentiable-programming

00-inbox/storm-sampling-mpc.title: STORM: An Integrated Framework for Fast Joint-Space Model-Predictive Control for Reactive Manipulation
00-inbox/storm-sampling-mpc.content:
Summary
Task-space MPC for manipulators cannot enforce joint-space constraints (limits, singularities, link collisions); gradient-based joint-space MPC requires differentiable costs, which breaks on contact. STORM runs sampling-based MPC (MPPI variant) directly in joint space by fully tensorizing the kinematic rollout for GPU parallelism, replacing pseudorandom control sampling with Halton+B-spline sequences for smoothness, and plugging learned collision checkers in as non-differentiable cost terms. The result runs at 125 Hz (8ms) on a single GPU — 100× faster than prior MPPI manipulation implementations — and is validated on a Franka Panda doing dynamic tracking and ball balancing.
Core Mechanism
Policy and update. The policy is a factored Gaussian over open-loop control sequences: \pi_{\phi_t} = \prod_{h=0}^{H-1} \pi_{\phi_{t,h}}, parameterized by means \mu_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times H} and covariances \Sigma_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times H}. The MPPI objective is the exponentiated utility:
L = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\phi, \hat{P}}\left[\exp\left(-\frac{1}{\beta}\hat{C}(x_t, u_t)\right)\right], \quad \hat{C} = \sum_{h=0}^{H-2} \gamma^h \hat{c}(\hat{x}_{t,h}, u_{t,h}) + \gamma^{H-1}\hat{q}(\hat{x}_{t,H-1})
Sample weights w_i = \exp(-\frac{1}{\beta}\hat{C}_i), then update:
\mu_{t,h} = (1-\alpha_\mu)\mu_{t-1,h} + \alpha_\mu \frac{\sum_i w_i u_{t,h}}{\sum_i w_i}, \qquad \Sigma_{t,h} = (1-\alpha_\sigma)\Sigma_{t-1,h} + \alpha_\sigma \frac{\sum_i w_i (u_{t,h}-\mu_{t,h})(u_{t,h}-\mu_{t,h})^\top}{\sum_i w_i}
Diagonal per-joint covariance \Sigma^U_{t,h} = \text{diag}(\sigma_1,\ldots,\sigma_d) replaces the scalar \sigma_u I of standard MPPI.
Kinematic rollout as a tensor operation. State x = [\theta, \dot\theta, \ddot\theta] \in \mathbb{R}^{3d}, control u = \ddot\theta. Integrating N particles over horizon H in batch:
\ddot\Theta = u, \quad \dot\Theta = \dot\theta_\text{init} + S_l^{(1)}\,\text{diag}(dt)\,\ddot\Theta, \quad \Theta = \theta_\text{init} + S_l^{(1)}\,\text{diag}(dt)\,\dot\Theta
where S_l^{(1)} is a lower-triangular ones matrix. The entire N \times H batch is a single tensor product — no Python loop over the horizon. Variable dt (small near current state, large later) gives high-resolution cost near now and better cost-to-go approximation.
Sampling strategy. Pseudorandom samples cluster; Halton sequences use coprime-base van der Corput sequences to achieve near-uniform coverage of \mathbb{R}^d. Controls are sampled once from Halton, then transformed by (\mu_t, \Sigma_t). B-spline degree-3 fitting to the Halton samples, then sub-sampled at finer resolution, produces smooth joint acceleration trajectories — the comb filter alternative requires manual coefficient tuning and doesn’t guarantee smoothness between adjacent horizon steps.
Cost terms (non-exhaustive):

Stopping contingency: time-varying velocity limit \dot\theta_{\max} = S_u^{(1)}\ddot\theta_{\max} dt; penalizes states that can’t brake by horizon end.
Singularity avoidance: \hat{c}_\text{manip} = 1 - \sqrt{\det J(\theta)J(\theta)^\top} when below threshold k_m.
Self-collision: jointNERF — MLP trained to predict minimum inter-link distance from [\sin\theta, \cos\theta] (positional encoding improves accuracy); cost = \max(0, \text{jointNERF}(\theta)).
Environment collision: learned binary classifier from Danielczuk et al., operating on raw pointcloud vs. robot link pointcloud; outputs \{0,1\} — no SDF, no gradient required.

Why It Matters
This lets you run joint-space MPC with non-differentiable perception inputs (raw pointclouds, binary collision classifiers) at real-time rates. Previously the bottleneck was twofold: (a) task-space MPC required a separate low-level controller that couldn’t account for joint limits or link collisions, forcing manual constraint engineering; (b) joint-space gradient-based MPC (iLQR/DDP) broke the moment costs were discrete or contact was present. STORM’s concrete payoff: a Franka tracking a human-moved ball through a cluttered scene, where the robot spontaneously backs off when the elbow would clip an obstacle, then re-approaches — without any replanning trigger, purely from the continuous cost gradient.
The ball-balancing task is the stress test: simplified gravity-only ball dynamics, no friction, 30Hz noisy perception. MPC’s receding-horizon re-optimization absorbs the model bias that would make an open-loop plan diverge in under a second.
Limitations

Kinematics-only model: at higher speeds, unmodeled inertia and joint flexibility accumulate error; the model bias that MPC re-optimization tolerates in slow tasks will not cancel fast enough for, say, high-speed throws or impacts.
No state uncertainty propagation: particle rollouts start from a point estimate x_t; state estimation noise is not propagated through the horizon. Under high observation noise (fast moving target + slow camera), the controller is effectively open-loop between perception updates.
Halton sequences are deterministic: the same N Halton points are used every cycle (transformed by current \mu, \Sigma). If the optimal control lies outside the current distribution’s support, the fixed Halton grid provides no escape — the algorithm can stall in local minima not recoverable by covariance adaptation alone.
Learned collision costs require offline training per robot: jointNERF must be retrained for each robot morphology; the environment pointcloud classifier also requires a training distribution that may not transfer to novel objects.
No hard constraint satisfaction: all constraints (joint limits, collision) enter as soft cost terms. High-weight penalties approximate constraint satisfaction but don’t guarantee it, particularly for sudden obstacles at close range within a single horizon.

Open Questions

Can a learned terminal Q-function (as suggested by the authors) replace the kinematic terminal cost and absorb model-bias due to dynamics, without losing the 8ms budget?
What is the right way to propagate state estimation covariance through the kinematic rollout — sigma-point / unscented transform seems tractable given the tensor structure, but what does it cost in wall-clock time?
Halton sequences fix coverage, but the distribution they’re drawn from (\mu_t, \Sigma_t) is still unimodal Gaussian — how badly does this fail on multimodal cost landscapes (e.g., two equally valid paths around an obstacle)?
The B-spline smoothing operates on joint accelerations but the cost is evaluated on joint positions — is there a formulation that directly samples smooth position trajectories (e.g., via trajectory optimization with smoothness priors) that would reduce the approximation gap?

Links

00-inbox/vic-mpc-humanoid-balance.title: Adaptive Disturbance Rejection Balance Control for Humanoid Robots via Variable-Inertia Centroidal MPC
00-inbox/vic-mpc-humanoid-balance.content:
Summary
Standard MPC for legged robots approximates the robot as a Single Rigid Body (constant inertia torso), which breaks down for humanoids where limb mass is a significant fraction of total mass and limb configuration actively changes the inertia tensor. This paper replaces the SRBM with a variable-inertia ellipsoid computed from the full kinematic chain each MPC cycle, transcribes the resulting OCP into a QP, and adds a dual-trigger stepping logic (Capture Point + Retrospective Horizon Average Centroidal Velocity) that avoids premature stepping on transient disturbances. Validated on the BHR8P humanoid (1.45m, 63.3kg), handling sagittal pushes up to 35 Ns and rotational platform disturbances at >2 Hz.
Core Mechanism
Centroidal dynamics with variable inertia. The robot’s whole-body inertia is collapsed into a single ellipsoid. The composite rigid-body inertia in the inertial frame is:
\mathbf{I}_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{n_b} {}^i\mathbf{X}_0^\top \mathbf{I}_i \,{}^i\mathbf{X}_0
The centroidal inertia \hat{\mathbf{I}}_G is extracted from the upper-left block of \mathbf{I}_0 after coordinate transform to the CoM frame (via Featherstone). This \hat{\mathbf{I}}_G is recomputed each MPC cycle from the current generalized coordinates q, then frozen for that cycle’s horizon — making B time-invariant within a cycle.
State and control vectors:
x = [c^\top, \theta_f^\top, l^\top, h^\top, g]^\top, \qquad u = [f_r^\top, \tau_r^\top, f_l^\top, \tau_l^\top]^\top
OCP → QP. Stage and terminal costs are quadratic penalties on centroidal state error and control effort. Constraints: (1) discrete linear dynamics x[k+1] = A_d x[k] + B_d u[k]; (2) Contact Wrench Cone (CWC) inequality, linearized as a 16-row polyhedral cone per foot encoding friction, CoP, and no-yaw-slip conditions:
\mathbf{C} \begin{bmatrix} \Re_r & 0 \\ 0 & \Re_l \end{bmatrix} u \leq 0
After horizon rollout, the full QP is:
\min_z \frac{1}{2} z^\top H_{qp} z + g_{qp}^\top z \quad \text{s.t.} \quad A_{ieq} z \leq b_{ieq}
with H_{qp} = 2(B_{qp}^\top \mathfrak{Q} B_{qp} + \mathfrak{R}). Solved with Eigen-QuadProg; typical solve time 1–2 ms, cycle time 5 ms.
Stepping trigger logic. Two complementary indices:

Capture Point (CP): \xi^{x,y} = c^{x,y} + \frac{1}{\omega}\dot{c}^{x,y}, where \omega = \sqrt{g/c^z}. Instantaneous; sensitive to noise.
RHACV: \hat{\dot{c}}^x(t_k) = \frac{1}{N^x}\sum_{i=0}^{N^x-1} \dot{c}^x(t_k - i\Delta t). Sliding-window average over \Delta T_{rh}. Smooths transient fluctuations.

Step triggered when both |\xi^x| > \xi^{x,th} and |\hat{\dot{c}}^x| > \hat{\dot{c}}^{x,th}_w (sagittal), or CP exceeds lateral bounds. Step terminates when RHACV in both planes falls below \hat{\dot{c}}^{th}_s = 0.025 m/s. Different retrospective horizons for sagittal (\Delta T^x_{rh} = 0.2s) and lateral (\Delta T^y_{rh} = 0.7s) — lateral gait alternation creates periodic velocity oscillations that require longer smoothing.
Joint torques from contact wrenches:
\tau_i^{jnt} = -J_i^\top \begin{bmatrix} {}^B R_0 & 0 \\ 0 & {}^B R_0 \end{bmatrix} w_i
Why It Matters
This lets you run centroidal MPC on a humanoid with significant limb mass (BHR8P legs are ~40% of total mass) without the model error that accumulates when you assume constant inertia. Previously, SRBM-based MPC either required heavy weight re-tuning whenever the robot changed posture (arm swing, crouching) or simply degraded gracefully until the robot fell. The RHACV addition specifically solves a field deployment problem: pure CP-based stepping triggers on every kick or sensor spike; RHACV confirms that the disturbance is sustained before committing to a step (which itself is destabilizing if unnecessary).
Limitations

Ellipsoid approximation neglects limb cross-inertia coupling: rapid leg swings or arm motions create angular momentum that the ellipsoid model doesn’t capture — errors accumulate during dynamic stepping at high speeds.
CWC linearization assumes flat foot contact: the 16-row polyhedral approximation to the friction cone degrades on uneven/sloped surfaces where the local contact normal rotates.
Manually tuned thresholds: CP and RHACV thresholds require per-platform tuning and were adjusted between simulation and hardware (e.g., CP threshold 0.1m sim → 0.08m hardware). No adaptive mechanism exists yet.
Horizon linearization: B and A are frozen within each MPC cycle. At longer horizons (N > 10) or higher velocities, accumulated linearization error will push the true trajectory outside the predicted tube.
No perception integration: foothold selection during stepping uses a fixed nominal position; terrain-aware reachability is described as future work.

Open Questions

Can \hat{\mathbf{I}}_G be predicted one step ahead (using a model of the swing leg trajectory) to reduce the latency between inertia change and MPC compensation?
How would you auto-tune the RHACV threshold online? Bayesian optimization over disturbance rejection trials seems tractable if you can define a scalar cost (fall rate, unnecessary step rate).
The RHACV window length for the lateral axis (0.7s) was chosen to average out step-induced oscillations — is there a principled frequency-domain argument (notch at the step frequency) that would generalize to other step periods?
Is the ellipsoid approximation improvable with a learned inertia residual, i.e., \hat{\mathbf{I}}_G^{\text{eff}} = \hat{\mathbf{I}}_G + \Delta I_\theta(q, \dot{q}), trained to minimize contact force tracking error?

00-inbox/wilson-confidence-interval.title: Wilson Confidence Interval for a Proportion
00-inbox/wilson-confidence-interval.content:
Summary
The Wald (textbook) confidence interval for a binomial proportion \hat{p} uses the estimated standard error in both the interval and the implicit hypothesis test, creating an inconsistency that produces pathological behavior near 0 or 1 and for small n. The Wilson interval fixes this by inverting the score test — using the null SE at the hypothesized value p_0 rather than at \hat{p} — yielding an asymmetric interval that cannot collapse to a point or overshoot [0,1]. It is the preferred method for small samples and extreme proportions.
Core Mechanism
The problem with Wald. The standard Wald interval is \hat{p} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \hat{\text{SE}}, where \hat{\text{SE}} = \sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/n}. The corresponding test for H_0: p = p_0 uses the null SE: \text{SE}_0 = \sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}. Because these two SEs differ, a value p_0 can be outside the confidence interval yet fail to be rejected by the test — a direct logical contradiction.
Additionally when \hat{p} = 0 or 1, \hat{\text{SE}} = 0 and the interval collapses to a single point with false certainty. When \hat{p} is near 0 or 1 with small n, the interval can extend outside [0,1].
Deriving the Wilson interval by test inversion. Construct the CI as the set of all p_0 that are not rejected by the score test at level \alpha. The score test rejects when:
\left|\frac{\hat{p} - p_0}{\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}}\right| > z_{\alpha/2}
Fail-to-reject region: square both sides and collect p_0 terms:
(\hat{p} - p_0)^2 \leq z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot \frac{p_0(1-p_0)}{n}
Expanding and rearranging into a quadratic in p_0:
(1 + \kappa)p_0^2 - (2\hat{p} + \kappa)p_0 + \hat{p}^2 \leq 0, \qquad \kappa = \frac{z_{\alpha/2}^2}{n}
Solving for the roots gives the Wilson interval:
\left[\frac{\hat{p} + \frac{\kappa}{2} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n} + \frac{\kappa}{4}}}{1 + \kappa}\right]
Concisely, let \tilde{n} = n + z^2 and \tilde{p} = (\hat{p}n + z^2/2)/\tilde{n} (a pseudo-proportion that “adds” z^2/2 successes and failures). Then:
\text{Wilson CI} = \tilde{p} \pm \frac{z}{\tilde{n}}\sqrt{n\hat{p}(1-\hat{p}) + z^2/4}
For \alpha = 0.05, z = 1.96 so z^2 \approx 3.84; the pseudo-count adds roughly 2 successes and 2 failures — which is exactly the Agresti-Coull adjustment. Agresti-Coull is thus a simplified approximation to Wilson that replaces the Wilson variance term with the Wald formula evaluated at \tilde{p}.
Key properties:

Interval endpoints are roots of a quadratic \Rightarrow always in (0,1) for 0 < \hat{p} < 1.
At \hat{p} = 0: lower bound = 0, upper bound = z^2/(n + z^2) > 0. Gives a non-trivial upper bound unlike Wald.
Asymmetric when \hat{p} \neq 0.5, which is correct since the binomial is asymmetric near its boundaries.
Coverage probability is consistently closer to the nominal (1-\alpha) than Wald, especially for small n and p near 0 or 1.

Continuity-corrected variant. Replace \hat{p} with (X \pm 0.5)/n in the variance term before inverting. Brings coverage closer to Clopper-Pearson (exact binomial) at the cost of wider intervals.
Why It Matters
This lets you report honest uncertainty bounds on a proportion when you have small samples or rare events — exactly the conditions where robotics success-rate experiments live. If you run 20 trials and succeed 19 times, Wald gives [0.79, 1.01] (nonsense upper bound clipped to 1), while Wilson gives approximately [0.72, 0.99]. More importantly, the Wald zero-width collapse at \hat{p} = 0/20 tells you nothing; Wilson gives a meaningful upper bound \approx 0.17 at 95% confidence. Any paper reporting success rates on physical hardware experiments with n < 50 should use Wilson or Agresti-Coull rather than the Wald interval.
Limitations

Wilson is still based on a normal approximation to the binomial. For very small n (say <10) and extreme p, Clopper-Pearson (exact) or Jeffreys (Bayesian) intervals are preferred because Wilson’s coverage can dip below nominal.
The interval is not the unique interval derivable from score-test inversion — it assumes a symmetric two-sided test; if you have a directional prior, a one-sided version should be used instead.
Continuity correction widens intervals, sometimes overcorrects, and is generally not recommended unless you specifically need conservative bounds.

Open Questions

When pooling success rates across multiple experiments with different n, how should Wilson intervals be combined? Meta-analytic variance-weighted approaches vs. random-effects models give different answers.
The Jeffreys interval (Bayesian with Beta(0.5, 0.5) prior) has slightly better coverage properties than Wilson for very small n — when is the crossover point where Wilson becomes preferable?

Links
binomial-distribution hypothesis-testing Clopper-Pearson Agresti-Coull score-test confidence-intervals Bernoulli-trials

10-robotics/algorithms/Spatial-Algebra-a.title: Spatial algebra and the Articulated-Body Algorithm
10-robotics/algorithms/Spatial-Algebra-a.content:
Summary
Forward dynamics on a kinematic tree — given (q, \dot q, \tau, f^{ext}), solve for \ddot q — can be done in O(n) without ever forming the joint-space inertia M(q). The Articulated-Body Algorithm (ABA) propagates a 6×6 articulated inertia I^A_i and a 6-vector bias p^A_i through the tree such that the inboard force satisfies f_i = I^A_i a_i + p^A_i, then unfolds joint accelerations in a third sweep. The construction rests on (i) Plücker spatial algebra (c.f. Pluecker-coordinates) with its motion/force duality, (ii) the body-frame EOM I\dot\nu + \nu \times^* I\nu = \phi^{ext}, and (iii) an induction over the tree that eliminates each \ddot q_c via the joint constraint \tau_c = S_c^\top f_c at the joint frame.
Notation and Conventions
Reference textbook: Featherstone, Rigid Body Dynamics Algorithms, Springer 2008

Body indices i = 1,\dots, N; \lambda (i) is the parent of body i; body 0 is the root/world.
\nu_i, a_i are the spatial velocity and accelerations in body i‘s frame (c.f. spatial-coordinates).
f_i is the spatial force on body i from \lambda (i), transmitted via the joint i (body-i frame)
S_i \in \mathbb R^{6\times d_i} is the motion subspace of joint i (e.g. (0,0,1,0,0,0)^\top for a z-axis revolute joint).
\tau_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d_i} is the active joint force (actuator torque).
{}^i X_j is the spatial motion transform from j to i.
{}^i X^*_j = ({}^j X_i)^\top is the dual force-vector transform.
I_i is the rigid-body spatial inertia of body i in its own frame.
\boldsymbol{c}_i = \nu_i \times S_i\,\dot q_i is the velocity-product bias acceleration. It appears because differentiating \nu_i = {}^i X_{\lambda(i)}\nu_{\lambda(i)} +    S_i\dot q_i in body coordinates produces this Coriolis-like term even when \ddot q_i = 0 and a_{\lambda(i)} = 0.
p_i = \nu_i \times^* I_i\,\nu_i - f^\text{ext}_i is the per-body bias force: the part of body i‘s Newton-Euler equation that doesn’t multiply a_i. It collects the gyroscopic/Coriolis wrench \nu \times^* I\nu and any external wrench applied to the body.

Core Mechanism
Spatial algebra primitives. A twist \nu = (\omega, v_O) \in \mathbb{R}^6 lives in motion space M^6; a wrench \phi = (n_O, f) \in \mathbb{R}^6 lives in dual force space F^6. Pairing \langle \phi, \nu \rangle = n_O^\top \omega + f^\top v_O is frame-invariant. Cross operators:
\nu\times = \begin{bmatrix} \hat\omega & 0 \\ \hat v_O & \hat\omega \end{bmatrix}, \quad \nu\times^* = \begin{bmatrix} \hat\omega & \hat v_O \\ 0 & \hat\omega \end{bmatrix}, \quad (\nu\times)^\top = -\nu\times^*.
Motion transforms {}^B X_A act on twists; force vectors use the dual {}^B X^*_A = ({}^A X_B)^\top.
Body-frame EOM. Newton’s law \dot h^0 = \phi^{ext,0} in the inertial frame pulls back via the structural identity
{}^0\dot X_B = {}^0 X_B\,(\nu^B \times),
which is the 6×6 adjoint analogue of R^\top \dot R = \hat\omega on SO(3). Transposing and dualizing yields {}^0\dot X^*_B = {}^0 X^*_B\,(\nu^B \times^*), hence the transport theorem for co-vectors:
\frac{d^0 h^0}{dt} = {}^0 X^*_B\Big( \frac{d^B h^B}{dt} + \nu^B \times^* h^B \Big).
Substituting h = I\nu with I constant in body coords gives
I\dot\nu + \nu \times^* I\nu = \phi^{ext}. \tag{NE}
The \nu\times^*I\nu term is not part of momentum — it is the correction for differentiating a co-vector in a frame that moves with the body. Restricting to pure rotation about the CoM (\nu = (\omega,0), I = \text{diag}(I_c, mI_3)) recovers I_c\dot\omega + \omega \times I_c \omega = \tau, the classical Euler equation.
Kinematic recurrence (Pass 1). For body i with parent \lambda(i):
\nu_i = {}^i X_{\lambda(i)}\,\nu_{\lambda(i)} + S_i \dot q_i, \quad a_i = {}^i X_{\lambda(i)}\, a_{\lambda(i)} + S_i \ddot q_i + \underbrace{\nu_i \times S_i \dot q_i}_{=: c_i}, \quad p_i = \nu_i \times^* I_i \nu_i - f^{ext}_i.
Body i‘s spatial Newton-Euler in body-i coords:
f_i - \sum_{c \in \text{ch}(i)} {}^i X^*_c\, f_c = I_i a_i + p_i. \tag{NE-tree}
Articulated-body relation, proved by induction leaves → root. Claim:
f_i = I^A_i a_i + p^A_i. \tag{$\star$}
Base case (leaf). No children, so (NE-tree) gives f_\ell = I_\ell a_\ell + p_\ell, hence I^A_\ell = I_\ell,\ p^A_\ell = p_\ell.
Inductive step. Assume (\star) for each child c. From Pass 1, a_c = {}^c X_i\, a_i + S_c \ddot q_c + c_c =: a'_c + S_c \ddot q_c. Substitute into f_c = I^A_c a_c + p^A_c:
f_c = I^A_c a'_c + U_c \ddot q_c + p^A_c, \quad U_c := I^A_c S_c.
Apply joint constraint \tau_c = S_c^\top f_c and use symmetry of I^A_c (so S_c^\top I^A_c = U_c^\top):
\ddot q_c = D_c^{-1}\big(u_c - U_c^\top a'_c\big), \quad D_c := S_c^\top U_c, \quad u_c := \tau_c - S_c^\top p^A_c.
Back-substitute to eliminate \ddot q_c from f_c:
f_c = \tilde I^A_c\, a'_c + U_c D_c^{-1} u_c + p^A_c, \quad \tilde I^A_c := I^A_c - U_c D_c^{-1} U_c^\top.
The -U_c D_c^{-1} U_c^\top term projects the free joint axis S_c out of I^A_c: on the direction the joint accommodates, no inertia is felt. Sum the children into (NE-tree) and identify with (\star) to read off the recurrences:
I^A_i = I_i + \sum_{c \in \text{ch}(i)} {}^i X^*_c\, \tilde I^A_c\, {}^c X_i \tag{R.1}
p^A_i = p_i + \sum_{c \in \text{ch}(i)} {}^i X^*_c\, \Big( \tilde I^A_c\, c_c + U_c D_c^{-1} u_c + p^A_c \Big) \tag{R.2}
Induction closes. ∎
The three passes.

























PassDirectionComputes1root → leaves\nu_i, c_i, p_i; init I^A_i \leftarrow I_i,\ p^A_i \leftarrow p_i2leaves → rootU_i, D_i, u_i, \tilde I^A_i; accumulate I^A_{\lambda(i)}, p^A_{\lambda(i)} via (R.1, R.2)3root → leavesa'_i = {}^i X_{\lambda(i)}\, a_{\lambda(i)} + c_i; \ddot q_i = D_i^{-1}(u_i - U_i^\top a'_i); a_i = a'_i + S_i \ddot q_i
Gravity is folded in by setting a_0 = -g. Each pass touches every link with fixed-size 6×6 operations: O(n) total.
CRBA cousin. (R.1) mirrors the CRBA recurrence I^C_i = I_i + \sum_c {}^i X^*_c\, I^C_c\, {}^c X_i. The only structural change is I^C_c \to \tilde I^A_c: CRBA locks every joint (children contribute full composite inertias), ABA frees them (the joint axis is projected out). One substitution distinguishes lock-everything inverse dynamics from let-everything-move forward dynamics.
RNEA duality. The inverse-dynamics relation that drops out is \tau_i = D_i \ddot q_i + U_i^\top a'_i + S_i^\top p^A_i. RNEA computes this given \ddot q; ABA inverts it for \ddot q given \tau.
Why It Matters
ABA is what makes real-time forward dynamics on a humanoid tractable. For a Unitree G1 (n \approx 23 joints), CRBA-plus-Cholesky is dominated by an O(n^3) linear solve — order 10^4 flops just for the inversion — while ABA is O(n) with no global matrix assembly, on the order of 10^2 per-link 6×6 operations. This is the kernel that lets a Whole-Body-MPC stack integrate a 23-DOF model at 1 kHz, that lets PPO/SAC training rollouts hit hundreds of thousands of env-steps per second on CPU, and that makes Pinocchio’s aba(model, data, q, v, tau) a hot inner loop rather than a bottleneck. The crossover where ABA beats CRBA-plus-Cholesky is n \gtrsim 9, well below any humanoid.
Pinocchio’s analytic derivatives — needed for DDP/iLQR-style trajectory optimization and for backprop through dynamics in differentiable simulation — differentiate through the three passes, which is only tractable because each pass is a local recurrence over fixed-size blocks. There is no M^{-1} Jacobian to invert.
The body-frame EOM derivation matters specifically because \nu \times^* I \nu is the most commonly bungled piece of multibody dynamics code. It is not a Lagrangian Coriolis term, it is a coordinate-transport correction; once you see it as the dual of R^\top \dot R = \hat\omega, the spatial form writes itself instead of degenerating into a stacked 3+3 mess that has to be re-derived every time the frame convention changes.
Limitations

Tree topology only. Closed loops (parallel five-bar legs, four-bar linkages, dual-arm grasping, a humanoid with both feet planted treated rigidly) break the parent-map recurrence. The standard fix — cut loops, run ABA on the spanning tree, project constraint forces via Lagrange multipliers — re-introduces an O(m^3) solve over m loop-closure constraints, eroding the O(n) win.
Floating-base inversion cost. For a 6-DOF floating base, D_0 is 6×6 and dominates the per-step constant factor. Most of the wall-clock budget on a humanoid sits in this single block, not in the joint chain.
Symmetry is exact-arithmetic-only. I^A_i is symmetric by induction (R.1 is a congruence sandwich, UD^{-1}U^\top is symmetric), but FP rounding breaks it across hundreds of recurrence updates. Production code symmetrizes explicitly each iteration.
No contact dynamics for free. ABA produces \ddot q for prescribed \tau and external wrenches at the link frames. Rigid unilateral contact (LCP with \lambda \geq 0, \phi \geq 0) sits outside ABA and is typically solved via a Delassus-matrix construction that calls ABA n_c times for n_c contact points.
Rigid bodies, joint actuators only. Reflected rotor inertia is a one-line patch (add \gamma_i^2 I_{\text{rotor},i} to D_i). Link flexibility, gear backlash, and series-elastic actuation are not.

Open Questions

Reverse-mode AD through ABA. Forward-mode AD is straightforward (push tangents through the three passes). Reverse mode requires reversing the topological order and the cross-pass data dependencies (Pass 3 reads I^A_i, U_i, D_i produced in Pass 2 from \nu_i, p_i produced in Pass 1). What’s the minimal checkpointing scheme, and how does AD-generated code for \partial \ddot q / \partial q, \partial \ddot q / \partial \tau compare to Pinocchio’s hand-derived analytic Jacobians on cost and accuracy?
Residual-learning injection point. Plausible hooks: per-link bias on p^A_i (additive correction, preserves O(n) structure and locality), per-joint multiplier on D_i^{-1} (multiplicative gain on response, also local but breaks linearity in \tau), or a whole-tree post-hoc \Delta \ddot q correction (loses interpretability and any structural prior). The per-link bias seems strictly dominant — worth verifying on a real sim-to-real gap dataset.
Centroidal-momentum fusion. Wensing & Orin (2016) compute A_G(q) in O(n) using the same articulated-inertia scaffolding. Is there a single fused sweep producing both \ddot q and A_G \dot q, or does the algebra force two distinct passes?
Floating-base D_0 structure. In the centroidal frame the 6×6 base inertia decouples into angular and linear blocks. Worth checking whether exploiting this drops the base-solve below its current ~200-flop cost on a humanoid.
Implementation sanity. Code ABA from scratch on a 3-link planar chain and verify \ddot q against M^{-1}(\tau - h) computed via CRBA + Cholesky (Exercise 4 of the deck). Worth doing once with naked spatial algebra, no Pinocchio, to internalize the recurrences.

Links
CRBA-composite-rigid-body-algorithm RNEA-recursive-newton-euler Pluecker-coordinates spatial-inertia centroidal-momentum-matrix Pinocchio-internals forward-dynamics contact-dynamics-LCP differentiable-physics se3-adjoint

10-robotics/control/Friction-Cone-Discre.title: Friction-Cone Discretisation
10-robotics/control/Friction-Cone-Discre.content:
Summary
The Coulomb friction model defines a convex cone of admissible contact forces; solving contact-rich QPs requires discretising this cone into either a linear pyramid approximation or an exact second-order cone. The choice propagates upward: pyramid errors in per-contact cones corrupt the Contact Wrench Cone (CWC) and therefore every centroidal feasibility check built on it. The deck gives constraint matrices, error bounds, and the wrench-level inequalities for a rectangular foot.
Core Mechanism
Coulomb cone. A contact force \mathbf{f} = f_n \hat{n} + \mathbf{f}_t is physically admissible iff
f_n \geq 0, \quad \|\mathbf{f}_t\|_2 \leq \mu f_n
This is a 3-D Lorentz cone \mathcal{L}^3 = \{(\mathbf{x}, t) : \|\mathbf{x}\| \leq t\}. The complementarity condition f_n \cdot \text{gap} = 0 enforces unilateral contact.
Linearised pyramid (LP form). Approximate the friction disc with a K-sided inscribed polygon:
\mathbf{f}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \beta_i \mathbf{d}_i, \quad \beta_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_i \beta_i \leq \mu f_n
\mathbf{d}_i = \mu\!\left(\cos\tfrac{2\pi i}{K},\, \sin\tfrac{2\pi i}{K}\right)
Inscribed polygon under-approximates (conservative); circumscribed over-approximates (relaxed). Approximation error for the inscribed case:
\cos(\pi/K) - 1 \approx -\frac{\pi^2}{2K^2}
K=4: ~30% error. K=8: ~8%. K=16: ~2%.
SOCP form (exact). The exact constraint is
(\mathbf{f}_t,\, \mu f_n) \in \mathcal{L}^3
Solved by primal-dual interior-point in O(N^{1.5} \log(1/\epsilon)). Solvers: ECOS, Mosek, OSQP-SOC. Overhead vs. pyramid: 5–10× slower per solve, but no K-knob bias.
Contact Wrench Cone (CWC) — rectangular foot. For a foot with half-extents (\ell_x, \ell_y), the necessary and sufficient wrench inequalities at the sole frame (Caron 2015):
f_z \geq 0
|f_x|,\, |f_y| \leq \mu f_z
|\tau_x| \leq \ell_y f_z, \quad |\tau_y| \leq \ell_x f_z
|\tau_z| \leq \mu_\tau f_z, \quad \mu_\tau = \mu(\ell_x + \ell_y)
Construction: take the convex hull of the union of per-corner friction cones, then express in wrench coordinates.
Multi-contact centroidal cone. The reachable centroidal wrench is the Minkowski sum of per-contact cones transformed to the CoM frame:
\mathcal{W} = \bigoplus_i {}^c\mathbf{X}^*_i \mathcal{K}_i
If every \mathcal{K}_i is polyhedral (pyramid discretisation), \mathcal{W} is a finitely-generated polyhedral cone computable via pycddlib/cdd-lib.
LCP formulation (Stewart–Trinkle). Time-stepping with linearised friction yields a mixed LCP:
M(q_k)v_{k+1} = M(q_k)v_k + h\left[\tau - C(q,v) - g\right] + h J_n^\top f_n + h J_t^\top \beta
with complementarities 0 \leq f_n \perp \phi_n \geq 0, 0 \leq \beta \perp E^\top f_n + \cdot \geq 0, 0 \leq \lambda \perp \mu f_n - E^\top\beta \geq 0. Solvable by Lemke’s algorithm. MuJoCo replaces this with a smooth convex relaxation for differentiability.
Why It Matters
In centroidal MPC for a bipedal robot, the feasibility constraint at each timestep is “does a wrench exist in the CWC?” If your CWC was built from K=4 pyramids, the MPC will consider some physically achievable wrenches infeasible (inscribed) or allow some that violate friction (circumscribed). The error shows up as a controller that either falls unnecessarily on high-\mu surfaces or issues torque commands the WBC can’t satisfy — not as a solver error, but as silent constraint mismatch between planning and execution.
Limitations

Pyramid approximation error is fixed at construction; it doesn’t adapt to contact geometry or \mu estimates.
The yaw friction moment bound \mu_\tau = \mu(\ell_x + \ell_y) is tiny for narrow feet (e.g. G1 where \ell_y \ll \ell_x), making yaw nearly uncontrollable through ground contact alone.
Minkowski sum of two SOCs is generally not an SOC — multi-contact SOCP cannot be collapsed to a single cone, requiring either enumeration or LP outer approximation.
LCP with Lemke is polynomial in the number of solution modes but can be slow on hardware; MuJoCo’s relaxation sacrifices physical accuracy for speed and differentiability.
CWC derivation assumes fixed contact positions; during transitions (heel-to-toe roll) the CWC changes shape mid-timestep.

Open Questions

Does the centroidal MPC’s inscribed pyramid systematically under- or over-promise control authority relative to the WBC’s exact SOC? The bias direction depends on whether the pyramid is inscribed or circumscribed — quantify this for your specific foot geometry.
For narrow-foot platforms where \mu_\tau is negligible, what is the minimum stepping bandwidth needed to recover yaw authority that the foot can’t provide?
Can a NN learn to predict the active pyramid corner (active constraint set) faster than the QP solves? What does the label space look like — one-hot over K faces, or a signed-distance to each face?
Is there a principled way to adapt K online based on estimated \mu and required wrench accuracy?

Links
contact-wrench-cone centroidal-mpc whole-body-control LCP-frictional-contact SOCP-solvers

10-robotics/control/HZD-and-Virtual-Cons.title: HZD and Virtual Constraints
10-robotics/control/HZD-and-Virtual-Cons.content:
Summary
Hybrid Zero Dynamics (HZD) provides the first formal stability proofs for periodic bipedal walking by reducing the full hybrid system to a low-dimensional zero-dynamics manifold and checking stability of the resulting Poincaré return map. Virtual constraints are output functions that pin the robot’s configuration to a designer-chosen curve parametrised by a phase variable, enforced via input-output feedback linearisation. The approach is conceptually orthogonal to centroidal MPC: it sacrifices online replanning for provable orbital stability.
Core Mechanism
Hybrid dynamical system. State x = (q, \dot{q}) \in TQ. Between impacts:
\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)u, \quad x \notin \mathcal{S}
At impact:
x^+ = \Delta(x^-), \quad x^- \in \mathcal{S}
The switching surface \mathcal{S} = \{x : h_{\text{swing}}(q) = 0,\; \dot{h}_{\text{swing}} < 0\}. The impact map \Delta is derived from a plastic collision conserving angular momentum about the new contact point; stance and swing roles swap.
Virtual constraints. Define output functions
h(q) = h_d(\theta(q)) - h_a(q)
where \theta(q) is a monotone phase variable (e.g. stance hip angle) and h_d(\theta) is a Bézier polynomial. Drive h \to 0 via IO feedback linearisation:
\ddot{h} = L_f^2 h + L_g L_f h \cdot u \implies u = (L_g L_f h)^{-1}(v - L_f^2 h)
v = -K_d \dot{h} - K_p h
This is a coordinate change that pins the robot to a 2-D submanifold of TQ parametrised by \theta.
Zero dynamics manifold.
\mathcal{Z} = \{(q, \dot{q}) : h(q) = 0,\; L_f h(q, \dot{q}) = 0\} \subset TQ
Once on \mathcal{Z}, the dynamics reduce to a single second-order ODE in \theta:
\mathcal{D}(\theta)\ddot{\theta} + \mathcal{C}(\theta, \dot{\theta})\dot{\theta} + \mathcal{G}(\theta) = 0
where \mathcal{D}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{G} are the Lagrangian coefficients pulled back to \mathcal{Z}. This admits a first integral (energy on \mathcal{Z}):
E(\theta, \dot{\theta}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathcal{D}(\theta)\dot{\theta}^2 + V(\theta)
A periodic orbit exists iff a closed level curve of E in the (\theta, \dot{\theta}) plane is consistent with the impact map \Delta.
Poincaré map and stability. Take a section \mathcal{S} just after each impact. The return map P : \mathcal{S} \to \mathcal{S} has a fixed point x^\star = P(x^\star) corresponding to the periodic orbit. Stability condition:
\text{all eigenvalues of } DP(x^\star) \text{ inside the unit circle}
On \mathcal{Z}, the restricted map is 1-D in \dot{\theta}, reducing to a single scalar eigenvalue check. Westervelt–Grizzle (2003): if the restricted map is stable and transverse dynamics converge to \mathcal{Z} exponentially fast enough, the full hybrid system has a stable periodic orbit.
Compass-gait example. Two-link planar walker, slope \alpha. Phase variable \theta = q_1 (stance angle). Virtual constraint h(q) = q_2 - h_d(\theta) with cubic Bézier h_d. Stability check:
|DP|_{\mathcal{Z}}| = \left(\dot{\theta}^+/\dot{\theta}^-\right)^2 < 1 \quad \text{at the fixed point}
Why It Matters
In centroidal MPC you get disturbance rejection for free — push recovery is just another QP query. What you give up is a formal guarantee that the gait is periodic and orbitally stable. HZD fills that gap: if you are deploying on hardware where a proof of stability matters (certification, safe autonomy) and the environment is structured (flat ground, known terrain), the Poincaré eigenvalue check is a concrete certificate that centroidal stacks cannot provide. Cassie and MABEL used HZD-derived gait libraries precisely for this reason.
Limitations

Virtual constraint design is per-robot and per-gait; transferring to a different morphology requires redesign.
IO linearisation is exact only if L_g L_f h is invertible — near torque saturation or at kinematic singularities this breaks, requiring a CBF-QP fallback.
HZD classically assumes point-foot or rigid-foot contact with an instantaneous impact; planar polygon feet (e.g. G1) complicate the impact map derivation without breaking the framework entirely, but require multi-domain extensions.
Push recovery requires adding a separate reflex layer or switching to MPC; it is not intrinsic to the HZD controller.
Gait library approach (Da et al. 2017) mitigates rigidity but the library must be pre-computed offline and the switching logic adds implementation complexity.

Open Questions

What is the formal relationship between the centroidal MPC’s linearisation manifold and HZD’s \mathcal{Z}? Both are reduced-order submanifolds of TQ — can one be embedded in the other?
Can virtual constraints be learned from motion-capture data rather than hand-designed? How does this compare to AMP/ASE which implicitly learn a reference manifold from demonstrations?
For a planar polygon foot (non-point contact), the impact map must account for the choice of pivot corner — does this produce a multi-valued \Delta that breaks the Poincaré analysis or just adds cases?
How does friction-cone discretisation enter the zero dynamics? Specifically, if the pyramid approximation over-promises friction, does the periodic orbit of the zero dynamics remain consistent with the true contact constraints?

Links
poincare-maps hybrid-systems centroidal-mpc CBF-QP compass-gait virtual-constraints

10-robotics/learning/Constrained-Policy-.title: Constrained Policy Optimization (CPO) and Safe RL
10-robotics/learning/Constrained-Policy-.content:
Summary
Reward-shaping penalties give no per-iteration safety guarantee and introduce an untunable scale hyperparameter. CMDPs formalize safety as hard budget constraints J_{c_i}(\pi) \leq d_i, and CPO solves each policy update as a trust-region QP that enforces those constraints while maintaining monotone reward improvement. The result is a policy update with O(\sqrt{\delta})-bounded expected constraint violation per iteration and a recovery rule for infeasible iterates.
Core Mechanism
CMDP formulation
\max_\pi J_r(\pi) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad J_{c_i}(\pi) := \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi}\!\left[\sum_t \gamma^t c_i(s_t, a_t)\right] \leq d_i, \quad \forall i
Surrogate + KL bound (the trust-region motor)
Performance-difference identity (Kakade–Langford):
J(\pi') - J(\pi) = \frac{1}{1-\gamma}\mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^{\pi'}, a \sim \pi'}\!\left[A^\pi(s,a)\right]
Replace d^{\pi'} with d^\pi (importance-sampled) to get the computable surrogate:
L_\pi(\pi') = \frac{1}{1-\gamma}\mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi, a \sim \pi}\!\left[\frac{\pi'(a|s)}{\pi(a|s)} A^\pi(s,a)\right]
with bound J(\pi') \geq L_\pi(\pi') - C\,\mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi}\!\left[\mathrm{KL}(\pi'\|\pi)(s)\right].
CPO update step (linearise objective and constraints, quadratic KL):
\theta_{k+1} = \arg\max_\theta\; g^\top(\theta - \theta_k)
\text{s.t.}\quad \tfrac{1}{2}(\theta-\theta_k)^\top F(\theta-\theta_k) \leq \delta
b_i^\top(\theta - \theta_k) + e_i \leq 0, \quad e_i := J_{c_i}(\theta_k) - d_i
where g = \nabla_\theta J_r, b_i = \nabla_\theta J_{c_i}, F = Fisher information matrix.
Stationarity \Rightarrow closed-form step:
\Delta\theta = \frac{1}{\nu} F^{-1}\!\left(g - \textstyle\sum_i \lambda_i b_i\right)
\nu, \{\lambda_i\} from a small dual QP (analytic for m=1). F^{-1}v computed via conjugate gradient — no matrix inversion.
Infeasibility recovery (when J_{c_i}(\pi_k) > d_i):
\Delta\theta^{\text{rec}} = -\sqrt{\frac{2\delta}{b_i^\top F^{-1} b_i}}\, F^{-1} b_i
i.e., the steepest KL-step that reduces the constraint. Theorem: violation bounded O(\sqrt{\delta}) per iteration.
GAE for advantage estimation (both reward and cost critics):
\hat{A}^{\mathrm{GAE}(\gamma,\lambda)}_t = \sum_{l=0}^\infty (\gamma\lambda)^l \delta_{t+l}, \quad \delta_t = r_t + \gamma V_\phi(s_{t+1}) - V_\phi(s_t)
PPO-Lagrangian (weaker, no per-iteration guarantee):
\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L} = \nabla_\theta J_r - \sum_i \lambda_i \nabla_\theta J_{c_i}, \quad \lambda_i \leftarrow [\lambda_i + \eta(J_{c_i} - d_i)]_+
Stooke 2020: replace scalar \eta with a PID controller on \lambda to suppress oscillation.
CBF safety filter (certificate view, orthogonal to RL):
u^\star = \arg\min_u \tfrac{1}{2}\|u - u_{\text{nom}}\|^2 \quad \text{s.t.}\quad L_f h(x) + L_g h(x) u + \alpha(h(x)) \geq 0
Discrete-time analogue: enforce h(x_{k+1}) \geq (1-\eta)h(x_k); becomes a QCQP when h is concave quadratic and dynamics are affine.
Why It Matters
On a humanoid (Unitree G1 class): torque limits, friction-cone margins, and fall avoidance are non-negotiable hard constraints, not “soft preferences.” Reward shaping with a penalty r - \beta c has no per-rollout guarantee — \beta is scale-dependent, tail violations are unbounded, and a single bad update can collapse the gait. CPO lets you train with J_c \leq d enforced in expectation at every gradient step, so the constraint budget is a tunable engineering parameter (e.g., d = 0.05 torque-saturation rate) rather than an implicit consequence of \beta. The CBF layer can then be stacked on top to handle per-step set-invariance (friction cone) without retraining.
Limitations

Linearisation error: g, b_i are first-order Taylor truncations of the true objective/constraint surfaces. The O(\sqrt{\delta}) bound degrades when curvature is high (dense contact transitions, discontinuous reward).
Expectation-level safety only: J_{c_i}(\pi) \leq d_i is an average. Rare catastrophic events (spike torques, stumble recovery) are not bounded in probability — CVaR or chance constraints would be needed for tail safety.
Fisher approximation cost: CG solve per update is O(n_\theta \cdot k_{\text{CG}}); expensive for large networks. PPO-Lagrangian is faster but weaker.
Stochastic policy requirement: CMDP optimal policy may be stochastic even when the unconstrained optimum is deterministic. Deterministic actor architectures (DDPG-style) are incompatible without modification.
No inter-sample guarantee for discrete CBF: the linearised discrete CBF is tight only at the prediction step; at 100 Hz actuation, inter-sample violation is non-negligible (moot at 1 kHz).

Open Questions

Residual-RL constraint placement: for a policy \pi = \pi_{\text{MPC}} + \delta\pi, constraining \delta\pi alone gives tighter formal bounds (MPC feasibility domain is known) but requires the MPC to be admissible at all times. Constraining total action is easier to implement but widens the feasible set.
CPO + CBF composition: CPO provides monotone improvement in expectation; CBF provides per-step set-invariance. The composition — run CPO offline, deploy with CBF filter online — is sound but the CBF distorts the action distribution the policy was trained under, creating a train/deploy mismatch. How large is that gap?
CVaR constraints in the CMDP: replacing J_{c_i} \leq d with a CVaR constraint changes the dual variable structure; the Lagrangian is no longer linear in the policy and the conjugate-gradient trick breaks. What’s the right surrogate?
Early termination vs. finite \lambda: ET = \lambda \to \infty kills gradient through the constraint manifold; Safety-Gym benchmarks show 3–5× slower learning. Is there a curriculum schedule \lambda(k) that recovers ET’s conservatism late in training while preserving CPO’s gradient flow early?

References
TODO
Links
TRPO PPO CMDP GAE Fisher-information-matrix control-barrier-functions Lagrangian-relaxation sim-to-real humanoid-locomotion-safe-rl
